title: practical english usage - Part 79
content:
Those is often used with a participle to mean ‘ the ones who  are/were'.

1 got the only ticket left, (     ... the only left ticket.) I watched the match because I knew some of the people playing.

questioned ...) (\oi  The questioned people . . .) The people questioned gave very different opinions. (= The  people who  were not were discussed.) (     .. .-the discussed problems.) We couldn't agree on any of the problems discussed. (= ... the problems that the same way as we  use identifying relative clauses (►   234).

We   often use participles after nouns in order to define or identify the nouns, in 6 after    nouns:    the    people        questioned a recently-built house home-made cake    the above-mentioned point quick-growing trees   government-inspired rumours Other compound structures with participles are also common before nouns.

a fox-hunting man  Is that watch self-winding?

English-speaking Canadians, (     speaktng-En^tsk-Ganadians.) Participles used as adjectives can have objects. Note the word order.

5 compound        structures:       English-speaking                Canadians The train Just arrived at platform six is the delayed 13.15 from Hereford.

a much-travelled man  recently-arrived immigrants but not a well-read person (          a-read-person) adverbials but not otherwise.

A  few past participles are used with active meanings in expressions with Why is that man stood in the doorway?

When I saw her she was sat on the ground crying.

Sat and stood are often used like this in informal speech in British English.

I'll be finished in a few minutes.  Those days are gone now.

We're camped in the field across the stream.

I hope you're fully recovered from your operation.

Why are all those cars stopped at the crossroads? (          .. .-a^stoppedreor) Some others are used after be, but not before nouns.

Those curtains are badly faded.

My family are all grown up.    My wrist is swollen.

She is retired now.         This class is the most advanced.

Most of these can be used after be.

grammar •  96  participles: -ing and -ed forms used like adjectives a shrunken head        rotten fruit/vegetables, etc drunken laughter/singing, etc       a sunken wreck/ship, etc certain expressions.

A  few older forms of past participles are still used as adjectives before nouns in 10 special     past    participle       forms:    drunken         laughter The hills are covered in snow. The room was filled with thick smoke.

His whereabouts are known to the police.

We were shocked at/by the prices in London.

- He was badly shocked by his fall.

I'm surprised at/by your attitude.

- The burglar was surprised by the family coming home unexpectedly.

I'm annoyed with you.

- I was annoyed by the way she spoke to me.

Joe's excited about the possibility of going to the States.

- The kids were so excited by the music that they kept screaming.

(Frightened is an adjective referring to a state of mind.) She's always been terribly frightened of dying.

(Frightened is part of a passive verb referring to an action.) - She was frightened by a mouse that ran into the room.

After past participles that are used like adjectives, we  prefer other prepositions.

Most of the damage was caused by your sister.

does the action, ►    58).

By is used after passive verbs to introduce the agent (the person or thing that 9 frightened           by   I frightened           of performance.

I was very amused / much  amused / very much  amused by Miranda's With amused, very and (very) much  are both possible.

He's well known in the art world, (     .. .-very-known-...) mistaken/) not That's Alice, unless I’m  (very) much  mistaken, (     ... ttnlrst Tm Common exceptions: She looked very surprised.

a very shocked expression The children were very bored.

a very frightened animal (     & much  frightened animal) common with words referring to mental states, feelings and reactions.

When  a past participle is used as an adjective, we  usually prefer very. This is noi (    .. .-very-weakened...) Britain's trade position has been (very) much  weakened by inflation.
